title: Ampere hour

infobox.value: or **amp-hour** (symbol: **A⋅h** or **A h**, often simplified as **Ah**) is a [unit](/wiki/Unit_of_measurement "Unit of measurement") of [electric charge](/wiki/Electric_charge "Electric charge"), recommended for use in [batteries](/wiki/Electrical_battery "Electrical battery") and [electrolytic](/wiki/Electrolytic "Electrolytic") devices.[[1]](#cite_note-IEV-1) It has [dimensions](/wiki/Dimensional_analysis "Dimensional analysis") of [electric current](/wiki/Electric_current "Electric current") multiplied by time and corresponds to the charge transferred by a steady current of one [ampere](/wiki/Ampere "Ampere") flowing for one hour (3,600 seconds). Thus 1 A⋅h equals 3600 A⋅s or 3.6 kC (kilo[coulombs](/wiki/Coulomb "Coulomb")).[[1]](#cite_note-IEV-1)[[2]](#cite_note-NIST-SI-Guide-2)
(symbol: **mA⋅h** or **mA h**, often simplified as **mAh**) is one-thousandth of an ampere-hour (3.6 C).

Thompson, Ambler; Taylor, Barry N. (2008). [*Guide for the Use of the International System of Units (SI). NIST Special Publication 811*](https://nvlpubs.nist.gov/nistpubs/Legacy/SP/nistspecialpublication811e2008.pdf) (PDF) (2nd ed.). Gaithersburg: National Institute of Standards and Technology. p. 45. "To convert from *ampere hour* (A·h) ... to coulomb (C) ... multiply by 3.6×103"

[X-ray Safety Handbook, 9.0 Terms and Definitions, VirginiaTech Environmental, Health and Safety Services](http://www.ehss.vt.edu/Programs/RSD/Xray/xray09.htm) [Archived](https://web.archive.org/web/20070723051053/http://www.ehss.vt.edu/Programs/RSD/Xray/xray09.htm) July 23, 2007, at the [Wayback Machine](/wiki/Wayback_Machine "Wayback Machine")
Efty Abir, Najrul Islam (2016). ["How to Calculate Amp Hours – Learn of Convert Watts to Amps"](http://trollingpowersolution.com/how-to-calculate-amp-hours/). Leo Evans. Retrieved 8 December 2016.

National Research Council (U.S.) (2004). *Meeting the energy needs of future warriors*. National Academies Press. p. 27. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [0-309-09261-2](/wiki/Special:BookSources/0-309-09261-2 "Special:BookSources/0-309-09261-2").

T. L. Brown, H. E. Lemay Jr, "Chemistry the Central Science", Prentice-Hall, 1977 [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [0-13-128769-9](/wiki/Special:BookSources/0-13-128769-9 "Special:BookSources/0-13-128769-9") page 562

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Ampere hour](/w/index.php?title=Ampere_hour&redirect=no "Ampere hour"))

Unit of electric charge

| ampere-hour | |
| --- | --- |
| Rechargeable NiMH 1.2 V batteries *Top*: AA battery (2500 mA⋅h) *Bottom:* AAA battery (1000 mA⋅h) | |
| General information | |
| [Unit system](/wiki/System_of_units_of_measurement "System of units of measurement") | Non-SI metric unit |
| Unit of | [Electric charge](/wiki/Electric_charge "Electric charge") |
| Symbol | A⋅h, A h |
| Conversions | |
| 1 A⋅h *in ...* | *... is equal to ...* |
|  | |
| [SI units](/wiki/SI_units "SI units") | 3600 C |

An **ampere-hour** or **amp-hour** (symbol: **A⋅h** or **A h**, often simplified as **Ah**) is a [unit](/wiki/Unit_of_measurement "Unit of measurement") of [electric charge](/wiki/Electric_charge "Electric charge"), recommended for use in [batteries](/wiki/Electrical_battery "Electrical battery") and [electrolytic](/wiki/Electrolytic "Electrolytic") devices.[[1]](#cite_note-IEV-1) It has [dimensions](/wiki/Dimensional_analysis "Dimensional analysis") of [electric current](/wiki/Electric_current "Electric current") multiplied by time and corresponds to the charge transferred by a steady current of one [ampere](/wiki/Ampere "Ampere") flowing for one hour (3,600 seconds). Thus 1 A⋅h equals 3600 A⋅s or 3.6 kC (kilo[coulombs](/wiki/Coulomb "Coulomb")).[[1]](#cite_note-IEV-1)[[2]](#cite_note-NIST-SI-Guide-2)

The commonly seen **milliampere-hour** (symbol: **mA⋅h** or **mA h**, often simplified as **mAh**) is one-thousandth of an ampere-hour (3.6 C).

## Use

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ampere-hour&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Use")]

The ampere-hour is frequently used in measurements of [electrochemical](/wiki/Electrochemistry "Electrochemistry") systems such as [electroplating](/wiki/Electroplating "Electroplating") and for [battery capacity](/wiki/Battery_capacity "Battery capacity") where the commonly known [nominal](/wiki/Real_versus_nominal_value "Real versus nominal value") [voltage](/wiki/Voltage "Voltage") is understood.

A *milliampere second* (mA⋅s) is a unit of measurement used in [X-ray imaging](/wiki/X-ray_imaging "X-ray imaging"), diagnostic imaging, and [radiation therapy](/wiki/Radiation_therapy "Radiation therapy"). It is equivalent to a *millicoulomb*. This quantity is proportional to the total X-ray energy produced by a given X-ray tube operated at a particular voltage.[[3]](#cite_note-3) The same total dose can be delivered in different time periods depending on the X-ray tube current.

To help express [energy](/wiki/Units_of_energy#Electricity "Units of energy"), computation over charge values in ampere-hour requires precise data of voltage: in a battery system, for example, accurate calculation of the energy delivered requires integration of the power delivered (product of instantaneous voltage and instantaneous current) over the discharge interval.[[4]](#cite_note-4) Generally, the battery voltage varies during discharge; an average value or nominal value may be used to approximate the integration of power.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

When comparing the energy capacities of battery-based products that might have different internal cell chemistries or cell configurations, a simple ampere-hour rating is often insufficient.

## In other units of electric charge

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ampere-hour&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: In other units of electric charge")]

One ampere-hour is equal to (up to 4 significant figures):

* 3600 [coulombs](/wiki/Coulomb "Coulomb")
* 2.247 × 1022 [elementary charges](/wiki/Elementary_charge "Elementary charge")
* 0.03731 [faradays](/wiki/Faraday_constant#Faraday_–_a_unit_of_charge "Faraday constant")
* 1.079 × 1013 [statcoulombs](/wiki/Statcoulomb "Statcoulomb") ([CGS-ESU](/wiki/Centimetre%E2%80%93gram%E2%80%93second_system_of_units#Electrostatic_units_(ESU) "Centimetre–gram–second system of units") equivalent)
* 360 [abcoulombs](/wiki/Abcoulomb "Abcoulomb") ([CGS-EMU](/wiki/Centimetre%E2%80%93gram%E2%80%93second_system_of_units#Electromagnetic_units_(EMU) "Centimetre–gram–second system of units") equivalent)

## Examples

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ampere-hour&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Examples")]

* An [AA size](/wiki/List_of_battery_sizes "List of battery sizes") [dry cell](/wiki/Dry_cell "Dry cell") has a capacity of about 2000 to 3000 milliampere-hours.
* An average smartphone battery usually has between 2500 and 6000 milliampere-hours of rechargeable electric capacity.
* Automotive [car starter batteries](/wiki/Car_battery "Car battery") vary in capacity but a large automobile propelled by an internal combustion gasoline engine would have about a 50-ampere-hour 12 V battery capacity.
* [Battery electric vehicle](/wiki/Battery_electric_vehicle "Battery electric vehicle") capacities are usually given in kW⋅h, but in 2013, the [BMW i3](/wiki/BMW_i3 "BMW i3") 60 A⋅h was named after the capacity of one of its 96 cells, for a total of 96 × 3.6 V × 60 A⋅h = 20736 W⋅h with about 18 kW⋅h usable energy, to match the number of the entry level [Tesla Model S](/wiki/Tesla_Model_S "Tesla Model S")60 which had 60 kW⋅h. Later BMW i3 had 94 A⋅h and 120 A⋅h batteries, each topping Tesla's S85, S90 and S100 designations.
* Since one ampere-hour can produce 0.336 grams of [aluminium from molten aluminium chloride](/wiki/Aluminium#Production_and_refinement "Aluminium"), producing a kilogram of aluminium required transfer of at least 2980 ampere-hours.[[6]](#cite_note-6) One kilogram of aluminium commonly requires 15.37 kW⋅h, thus electric power represents about 20% to 40% of the cost of producing aluminium.

## See also

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ampere-hour&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: See also")]

* [Electrochemical equivalent](/wiki/Electrochemical_equivalent "Electrochemical equivalent")
* [Kilowatt-hour](/wiki/Kilowatt-hour "Kilowatt-hour") (kW⋅h)
